retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Paramyxovirus -- Evaluation -- Respiratory Syncytial Virus
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis is clinical Nasal rapid antigen testing and PCR testing can be used should management change The chest x-ray may show hyperinflation of the lungs and patchy atelectasis [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Rubeola (Measles)(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Measles is a helical symmetry virus with non-segmented negative polarity ribonucleic acid (RNA), a member of the Paramyxoviridae family and the morbillivirus genus. It contains approximately 15,894 nucleotides, encoding e8 viral proteins within 6 genes (nucleoprotein [H], phosphoprotein [P], matrix [M], the fusion protein [F], hemagglutinin [H], and the polymerase [L]) as well as having an RNA-bound RNA polymerase. The HN glycoprotein has neuraminidase and hemagglutinating activities in different places of the same molecule, which explains the absorption and lysis of the host receptors. The fusion glycoprotein is responsible for viral penetration into the host cell, as it stimulates the fusion of viral and cellular membranes. The matrix forms the basis of the lipid coat. The gene P encodes the messenger RNAs for the C and V proteins, and these proteins have involvement in the regulation of host cell immune response. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Infection in Children -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: RSV is a single-stranded, negative-strand, RNA virus belonging to the Paramyxoviridae family, and is in the genus Pneumovirus . RSV was discovered in chimpanzees in 1955 and subsequently confirmed to be a human pathogen shortly after that. There are several animal respiratory syncytial viruses in the same genus as human RSV, which do not infect humans, and which will not be further referenced in this article. The structure of RSV is that of a bilipid-layer-envelope surrounding a ribonucleoprotein core, with several membrane proteins, one of which functions in attachment to host cells, and one of which functions in fusion to host cells. There is only one serotype of RSV, but it is classified into two strains, "A" and "B," with differences consisting of variation in the structure of several structural membrane proteins, most especially the attachment protein. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Parainfluenza Virus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: When the infection of epithelial cells of the small airways occurs, inflammatory infiltrates develop, and the host immune response is thought to contribute to disease pathogenesis. [1] The first morphologic changes seen when a cell is infected with the HPIV, are the cytoplasm and nucleus increasing in size, focal rounding, and the host cell mitotic activity is decreased as soon as 24 hours after inoculation of the HPIV. [3] Basophilic or eosinophilic inclusions can be found as well, as more changes, including single or multilocular cytoplasmic vacuoles and the formation of multinucleated giant cells, are noticed. [3] These giant cells are generally observed late in the infection, and they contain between two and seven nuclei. Paramyxoviruses are one family of pathogens that cause apoptosis in tissue culture cells. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Paramyxovirus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Paramyxoviruses are enveloped, single-stranded negative-sense RNA viruses that replicate in the cytoplasm.  Diseases caused by these viruses continue to produce high mortality and morbidity across the world. [1] With the development and use of vaccinations and medications, the incidence of serious illness due to paramyxoviruses has tremendously decreased. Yet despite the availability, given the freedom and choice of receiving pre and/or post-exposure treatment, the cases have increased even in developed countries. Cultivating effective vaccines is still in progress for some of the paramyxoviridae species. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Measles -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The causative organism of measles is the measles virus, an enveloped, single-stranded, negative-sense ribonucleic acid (RNA) virus belonging to the genus Morbillivirus in the family Paramyxoviridae (see Image. Paramyxovirus Virion, Transmission Electron Micrograph). The genome encodes 6 structural proteins and 2 non-structural proteins, V and C. The structural proteins are nucleoprotein, phosphoprotein, matrix, fusion, haemagglutinin (HA), and large protein. The HA protein is responsible for virus attachment to the host cell. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Paramyxovirus -- Evaluation -- Parainfluenza Virus (Croup)
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis is typically clinical [7] The chest x-ray may show a narrowing of the trachea, also known as the "steeple sign" [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus in Adults -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: RSV is a single-stranded, enveloped ribonucleic acid (RNA) virus approximately 15.2 kb in length. This virus belongs to the genus Pneumovirus , subfamily Pneumovirinae , and order Mononegavirales; filamentous forms of the virions can range up to 10 μm in length and 300 μm in diameter. [10] Structural features unique to RSV include helical striations surrounding its core, located internally within its envelope. The viral envelope contains glycoproteins G, F, and SH, which participate in viral attachment (protein-G), fusion and syncytia formation (protein-F), and ion channel function (protein-SH). Protein-F is the antigen target for currently approved RSV vaccines and monoclonal antibodies. Antigenic virus subtypes A and B do not appear to differ significantly in pathogenicity or protein-F antigenicity. [11] [12] The viral matrix protein plays a crucial role in forming stable virion structures. [13] The viral replication and transcription process is mediated through the RNA polymerase complex, a target for antivirals. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Paramyxovirus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The family Paramyxoviridae contains two subfamilies that are relevant to humans: Pneumovirinae and Paramyxovirinae. The subfamily Pneumovirinae gives rise to the genus Pneumovirus (respiratory syncytial virus).  The subfamily Paramyxovirinae gives rise to the genus Morbillivirus (measles virus/ rubeola), to the genus Respirovirus (para-influenza viruses 1 and 3), and to the genus Rubulavirus (mumps virus and para-influenza viruses 2 and 4). [3] Measles and rubella are targeted for elimination in five different World Health Organization (WHO) regions by 2020.

retrieved_docs.title: Paramyxovirus -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: RSV : RSV typically causes upper respiratory symptoms, which may including, fever, chills, myalgia, rhinorrhea, congestion, and cough. Occasionally, conditions could progress to lower respiratory infections that leads to bronchiolitis and in severe cases, viral pneumonia with dyspnea, usage of accessory muscles, and wheezes. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Infection in Children -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histopathology does not play a significant role in the diagnosis of RSV and the findings in mild disease are not known, but histopathologic findings of severe disease include abundant respiratory epithelial cell death, airway edema, and immune cell infiltration, initially polymorphonuclear early in the illness, and later in the illness, lymphomononuclear.

retrieved_docs.title: Poxviruses -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Structurally all the Poxviridae are massive, maintaining a brick-shaped proteolipid envelope further packed with even more “mature virion” inside; each one sporting their very own fusion-ready envelope - infectious in their own right (albeit with a differing route). Here the DNA is housed in a protein core, surrounded by its assortment of transcriptional machinery, even including a viral variant of DNA-RNA polymerase. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Filovirus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Structurally, filoviruses obtained their name due to their macroscopic appearance as filamentous viruses, from the Latin "filum" for "thread," often having a string-like or torus-like appearance.  They have enveloped particles that contain a negative-sense, single-stranded RNA genome with genes arranged in a linear fashion. Additionally, they have a single glycoprotein spike on the surface and 4 structural proteins, which include the virus-encoded polymerase. [1] Filoviruses are highly contagious and spread quickly via human-to-human contact. They are considered biosafety level 4 agents with very high mortality rates. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Evaluation -- Molluscum Contagiosum
retrieved_docs.content: Molluscum contagiosum is diagnosed histopathologically. The lesions demonstrate Henderson-Paterson bodies and inclusion bodies in cytoplasm, which are classic findings. Viral identification in the specimen is then performed with in situ hybridization. [11] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Rubella -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Rubella virus, the sole member of the genus Rubivirus in the Marnaviridae family, infects only humans. [8] [9] The virus is a spherical, enveloped, single-stranded, positive-sense RNA virus, with a genome of approximately 9.7 kb (see Image . Paramyxovirus Virion, Transmission Electron Micrograph). The viral genome encodes 2 nonstructural proteins (p90 and p150) and 3 structural proteins: glycoproteins (E1 and E2) and the capsid protein (see Image . Rubella Virus, Transmission Electron Micrograph).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1552
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a type of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (neonatal disorder). The broader category اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة includes the specific conditions كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة. Virologie is an allowed qualifier for اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, for respiratory distress syndrome, and also for كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة, indicating that viral factors may be relevant in the assessment and management of these neonatal disorders. This hierarchical organization links respiratory distress syndrome to other neonatal conditions and emphasizes the potential role of virology across the spectrum of neonatal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2048
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier indicates that virologie is an appropriate qualifier for the entities اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية, and رضح الولادة. اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة is described as having the child entities كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة, meaning that the neonatal disorder encompasses both excess fetal erythroblasts and the process of birth delivery. Consequently, virologie can be applied as a qualifying descriptor to the overall neonatal disorder as well as to each of its specific components, كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة. This relationship highlights the relevance of virological considerations in the assessment and classification of the neonatal disorder and its associated sub‑conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 5 -- Community 2944
retrieved_docs.content: In embryologie, the designation “Allowed qualifier” is used to encompass several developmental conditions, specifically عدوى بفيروس معوي (intestinal viral infection), تضيُّق الصمام المترالي (mitral valve stenosis), and فالج (paralysis). This classification indicates that these entities are recognized as permissible qualifiers when describing embryological abnormalities or variations, underscoring the range of potential fetal developmental issues and the need for their identification in medical evaluation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: antiviral therapy (e.g., telaprevir). Together, these entities illustrate a comprehensive therapeutic landscape that integrates hierarchical classification, mechanistic specificity, drug‑interaction considerations, diagnostic imaging agents, and a spectrum of antiviral modalities—from nucleoside analogues and entry inhibitors to monoclonal antibodies—each with defined indications and roles essential for optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1495
retrieved_docs.content: The term “vakcíny” is classified under the broader category of viral vaccines, indicating that viral vaccines serve as the parent classification for these vaccines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 830
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals are classified as direct acting antivirals, indicating that this group of cyclic amine compounds belongs to the broader class of antiviral agents that directly inhibit viral replication by targeting specific viral enzymes or proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2800
retrieved_docs.content: In the field of virology, the concept of an allowed qualifier is applied to the terms التموت and ورم عضلي أملس, indicating that these Arabic descriptors are recognized as permissible qualifiers within virological contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 280
retrieved_docs.content: Direct acting antivirals are classified as a subset of antivirals for systemic use, indicating that these agents are administered systemically and act directly on viral targets. This relationship places direct acting antivirals within the broader category of systemic antiviral therapies, helping clinicians recognize that they belong to the larger group of antivirals intended for systemic application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2679
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a permissible descriptor that can be applied in diagnostic, rehabilitative, and virological contexts to a wide range of conditions. In the diagnostic domain, it is used for وضعية المَجيء المقعدي, cardiac and circulatory congenital anomalies, التهاب وريدي, الشهوة الجنسية, ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ, ورم عضلي أملس, nervous system congenital anomalies, الالتصاق, انفصال الشبكية, and ضربة شمس. In rehabilitation, the same qualifier is employed for nervous system congenital anomalies, الشهوة الجنسية, ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ, ورم عضلي أملس, الالتصاق, انفصال الشبكية, and ضربة شمس. Virologically, Allowed qualifier is applicable to وضعية المَجيء المقعدي, cardiac and circulatory congenital anomalies, التهاب وريدي, الشهوة الجنسية, ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ, ورم عضلي أملس, nervous system congenital anomalies, الالتصاق, انفصال الشبكية, and ضربة شمس, indicating relevance of viral assessment for these entities. The integrated use of Allowed qualifier across these three domains underscores its broad applicability for patient assessment, therapeutic planning, and infectious disease evaluation across diverse organ systems and clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 5 -- Community 2941
retrieved_docs.content: In the context of virology, the notion of an “Allowed qualifier” is linked to three distinct clinical entities: ورم عضلي أملس (smooth muscle tumor), اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), and رضح الولادة (post‑partum lactation). This relationship indicates that, within virological frameworks, these conditions are recognized under a permissible qualifier, suggesting that virological considerations or classifications may be relevant when evaluating smooth muscle tumors, tongue disorders, and lactation changes after birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: The notion of an “Allowed qualifier” within the field of virologie designates specific Arabic terms that are recognized as permissible descriptors in virological contexts. In particular, the expressions “قيلة مثانية” and “ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ” are identified as allowed qualifiers in virologie, indicating that these phrases can be used to qualify or describe virology‑related observations or conditions. Recognizing these qualifiers helps ensure precise terminology and consistent communication when documenting virological findings that involve the concepts represented by “قيلة مثانية” and “ضُمورُ الكَبِد الأصفر الحادّ”.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 673
retrieved_docs.content: Neuraminidase inhibitors are classified as direct‑acting antivirals, meaning they target viral enzymes essential for replication. Zanamivir is a specific member of the neuraminidase inhibitor class and therefore also functions as a direct‑acting antiviral. By inhibiting the neuraminidase enzyme of the influenza virus, zanamivir may be used to prevent الإنفلونزا (influenza), highlighting its role in both antiviral therapy and prophylaxis against this common respiratory infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 395
retrieved_docs.content: disorder (اضطراب مخي وعائي), reflecting that these sequelae constitute a subset of broader cerebrovascular disorders. Collectively, these hierarchical and cross‑disciplinary relationships organize diagnoses by linking specific entities such as congenital vascular dilatation, late cerebrovascular sequelae, and rectal pathology to their larger disease families, underscoring the importance of integrated surgical, pathophysiological, pathological and virological perspectives in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
retrieved_docs.content: Zanamivir is classified as a member of the neuraminidase inhibitors, which are direct‑acting antivirals. As a neuraminidase inhibitor, zanamivir targets the viral neuraminidase enzyme, thereby blocking the release of progeny influenza viruses and inhibiting viral replication. This relationship places zanamivir within the broader group of direct‑acting antiviral agents that act specifically on viral proteins to treat infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2037
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a term used in radiotherapy to denote specific clinical conditions that are considered permissible or relevant when evaluating a patient for treatment. The allowed qualifiers for radiotherapy include respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest in adults, as well as عدوى بفيروس معوي (infection with an enteric virus). Recognizing these qualifiers helps clinicians assess patient suitability for radiotherapy, anticipate potential complications, and tailor therapeutic plans accordingly.
